item.title: #1894286: System updates are executed without priority
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Critical bug report
  • Version: 11.3.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: b090f8e · +186/-0 prod · +684/-0 test · 9 files
  • Discussion: #1894286 · 22 contributors · 108 comments

Summary

Drupal's update system did not always run system module database updates before other modules' updates, even when no explicit dependencies prevented it. This could cause cryptic failures during major or minor version upgrades, particularly when system updates made foundational changes that other updates depended on. The update dependency graph now ensures system updates execute as early as possible while still respecting explicit dependencies declared via hook_update_dependencies(). Sites performing upgrades across Drupal versions with many pending updates will now have more reliable update paths.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The update_build_dependency_graph() function in core/includes/update.inc now manipulates the dependency graph to prioritize system module updates. It tracks the highest numbered system update and the lowest numbered update for each non-system module.

When there are no explicit dependencies forcing non-system updates before system updates, the code adds edges from the last system update to the first update of every other module. This simple case ensures all system updates run first.

When hook_update_dependencies() forces non-system updates before system updates, the code builds an interim graph using Graph::searchAndSort(). It examines the paths key for each non-system update to find which system updates depend on it. Non-system updates with no system update dependencies get an edge from the last system update. Non-system updates that must run before a specific system update get an edge from the previous system update, preserving the dependency chain while still running as many system updates as early as possible.

The new UpdateOrderingTest unit test provides comprehensive coverage of update ordering scenarios including dependency chains, in-between dependencies, and complex multi-module dependency graphs. The test uses a fixture that implements hook_update_dependencies() with configurable return values.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: January 21, 2013
  • Committed: February 16, 2026 (13 years later)

Key contributors

  • larowlan (PreviousNext)
  • chx
  • hchonov (bio.logis Genetic Information Management GmbH)
  • tstoeckler
  • kfritsche (bio.logis Genetic Information Management GmbH)
  • gease (bio.logis Genetic Information Management GmbH)
  • gnunes (bio.logis Genetic Information Management GmbH)
  • alexpott (Acro Commerce)
  • andypost (Skilld)
  • dww (TEN7)
  • daffie
  • spokje
  • taran2l (Lemberg Solutions)
  • danielveza (PreviousNext)
  • catch (Third and Grove)

Collaboration

This issue remained open for over 13 years with 108 comments. hchonov identified reproducible cases where taxonomy updates ran before system updates during 8.4.8 to 8.6.x upgrades and provided early patches with graph manipulation logic. alexpott shepherded the final implementation through multiple iterations, refactoring the approach to use an interim graph object instead of complex recursive processing, and adding comprehensive test coverage. catch and larowlan provided thorough reviews that pushed the code toward clarity and correctness. The persistence across many contributors and the focus on test coverage for such a critical subsystem exemplify the care required for foundational Drupal systems.

Lessons learned

  • When building dependency graphs for ordered execution, implicit dependencies between subsystems can be as important as explicit ones. If framework updates must complete before application code updates run, add edges to enforce ordering even when developers haven't declared dependencies, because the dependency graph algorithm only respects edges, not module roles or semantics. (via hchonov, alexpott)
  • When a topological sort produces an acceptable but suboptimal ordering, manipulating weights after sorting is fragile. Instead, add missing edges to the dependency graph before sorting so the graph structure itself encodes the desired constraints and the sort algorithm naturally produces the correct order. (via hchonov, alexpott)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3566801: Helper to check if the entity type's ID is integer
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 747ad62 · +146/-124 prod · +82/-12 test · 38 files
  • Discussion: #3566801 · 6 contributors · 28 comments

Summary

Drupal core had repetitive code scattered across many modules to check whether an entity type uses integer IDs. This matters because integer IDs enable routing optimizations and affect how entity references are exported. The new helper method centralizes this logic, making code cleaner and easier to maintain. Three deprecated functions remain temporarily for backward compatibility but will be removed in Drupal 13.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • New API: New EntityTypeInterface::hasIntegerId() method checks if an entity type uses an integer ID field.

Technical details

This change adds EntityTypeInterface::hasIntegerId() which returns TRUE for integer IDs, FALSE for string IDs, or NULL if the entity type lacks an ID key. The base EntityType class returns FALSE (for config entities). ContentEntityType inspects the ID field definition to determine the actual type.

The method replaces repeated checks like $field_definitions[$id_key]->getType() === 'integer' throughout core. It's used in DefaultHtmlRouteProvider for route requirements, EntityReferenceItem for property definitions, Exporter for default content serialization, and WorkspaceTracker for ID field selection.

Three helper methods are deprecated: _comment_entity_uses_integer_id(), DefaultHtmlRouteProvider::getEntityTypeIdKeyType(), OverridesSectionStorage::hasIntegerId(), and CommentTypeForm::entityTypeSupportsComments(). The deprecated getEntityTypeIdKeyType() method in DefaultHtmlRouteProvider remains protected, so only extenders of that class are affected by the deprecation. The comment module function was public but rarely used outside core.

Upgrade

If you extended DefaultHtmlRouteProvider and called its protected getEntityTypeIdKeyType() method, replace it with $entity_type->hasIntegerId(). If you called the Comment module's _comment_entity_uses_integer_id() function, use \Drupal::entityTypeManager()->getDefinition($entity_type_id)->hasIntegerId() instead. The deprecated methods will be removed in Drupal 13. See the change record for details: #3566814.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: January 10, 2026
  • Committed: February 16, 2026 (1 month later)

Collaboration

Claudiu Cristea opened this issue as part of the broader effort to eliminate .module files and drove the implementation through multiple iterations. Nicxvan and smustgrave discussed the scope and confirmed that despite touching many test files, most changes were simplifications that justified keeping the work together. Berdir provided detailed review feedback and noted the value of having the method available on config entity types too, even though they always return FALSE. Catch committed the change after confirming that the lack of dependency injection was acceptable for now.

Lessons learned

  • When multiple parts of a system perform the same conditional check based on underlying data structure, extract the check into a shared API method on the data structure itself. This centralizes the logic, eliminates duplication, and makes the check discoverable through the type system. (via claudiu.cristea)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3540105: Capture access cacheability in getDefaultOperations() methods for all relevant entity list builders
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal bug report
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 502a59b · +206/-60 prod · +178/-18 test · 44 files
  • Discussion: #3540105 · 3 contributors · 15 comments

Summary

Entity list pages show operations like "Edit" and "Delete" for each entity row. These operations depend on access permissions and other dynamic factors. Drupal's cache system needs to know what conditions affect these operations so it can serve cached pages correctly. This fix ensures that when list builders check permissions or other conditions to build their operation links, they properly record those dependencies. Without this fix, cached entity list pages could show incorrect or stale operation links to users.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

A previous issue (#2473873) added support for passing a CacheableMetadata object to list builder operation methods so that cacheability information from access checks could be captured. However, many list builder subclasses override getDefaultOperations() or getOperations() but did not implement the necessary code to retrieve and pass along this optional second parameter.

This fix updates approximately 25 list builder classes across core modules to properly capture the cacheability parameter using func_get_args() and pass it to parent method calls. The pattern is consistent across all implementations: extract the $cacheability argument from the second position, defaulting to a new CacheableMetadata object if not provided, then pass it to parent::getDefaultOperations() or parent::getOperations().

Three list builders perform access checks within their getDefaultOperations() methods: FieldConfigListBuilder, VocabularyListBuilder, and WorkspaceListBuilder. These now call access() with return_as_object: TRUE to get an AccessResultInterface object, then add it to the $cacheability object via addCacheableDependency(). This ensures that cache tags, contexts, and max-age from access checks are properly captured.

Test coverage expanded significantly in EntityOperationsCacheabilityTest using a data provider to verify correct cacheability capture for entity types including blocks, comments, fields, filters, images, menus, nodes, search pages, shortcuts, taxonomies, users, views, and workspaces.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: August 6, 2025
  • Committed: February 16, 2026 (6 months later)

Key contributors

  • godotislate
  • mxr576 (Pronovix)
  • catch (Third and Grove)

Collaboration

godotislate opened this issue as follow-up work from #2473873, identifying the gap in cacheability support across list builder subclasses. They prepared the merge request with comprehensive fixes across 25+ list builder classes and significantly expanded test coverage to verify the changes work correctly for most entity types. mxr576 reviewed the changes and confirmed the test-only build failed as expected, validating that the tests properly detect the bug.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #2722307: Move translation based conditions into database query on revisions overview page
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal bug report
  • Version: 11.3.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 9367e55 · +294/-279 prod · +207/-30 test · 15 files
  • Discussion: #2722307 · 11 contributors · 57 comments

Summary

The revision history page for content could show incorrect pagination or even appear empty when a node had many translation-related revisions. The page would load 50 revisions from the database but then filter them afterwards, sometimes leaving nothing to display. The revision page now filters revisions directly in the database query so pagination works correctly and always shows the appropriate revisions for the current language.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The revision overview pages for nodes and other entities were loading revisions via a database query and then filtering them in PHP by checking isRevisionTranslationAffected(). This approach caused pagination problems because the pager would request 50 revisions from the database, but many could be filtered out afterwards, resulting in fewer revisions displayed than expected or even empty pages.

The fix moves the translation filtering logic into the database queries themselves. Both NodeController::getRevisionIds() and VersionHistoryController::loadRevisions() now add conditions for langcode and revision_translation_affected directly to the entity query before pagination is applied. This ensures the pager works correctly because it only counts revisions that will actually be displayed.

The changes remove the nested conditionals that checked hasTranslation() and isRevisionTranslationAffected() after loading revisions. These checks are now redundant because the database query already filters to affected revisions for the current language. The code is also simplified by removing unnecessary indentation levels in NodeController::revisionOverview().

Test coverage was added to verify that creating many non-affecting revisions does not break pagination, and that the pager correctly splits affected revisions across multiple pages.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: May 10, 2016
  • First commit: February 13, 2026 (9 years later)
  • Last commit: February 15, 2026 (2 days later)

Key contributors

  • mkalkbrenner (bio.logis Genetic Information Management GmbH)
  • catch (Third and Grove)
  • berdir (MD Systems GmbH)
  • yoroy (Roy Scholten)
  • acbramley (PreviousNext)
  • smustgrave (Mobomo)
  • liam morland (OpenPlus)
  • quietone (PreviousNext)
  • alexpott (Acro Commerce)

Collaboration

Adam Bramley drove this issue forward over several years, working through the complex interactions between revisions, translations, and pagination. The issue remained open from 2016 until 2026 as the community debated the right approach. Alex Pott updated the issue summary to match the final solution and committed the fix across multiple branches. Rafael Payan made their first commit to the issue fork. Multiple reviewers including smustgrave, quietone, and rosk0 tested the changes and pushed for clarity.

Lessons learned

  • When filtering data in memory after fetching from the database, pagination breaks if filtered items exceed the page size. Apply filters directly in database queries so pagination counts reflect actual displayable results. (via liam morland, acbramley)
  • When loading entities for display, filtering in memory after the query creates confusing user experiences where the 'current' state doesn't match reality. Move filtering logic into queries and surface actual system state, even if it requires additional UI to help users interpret the results. (via catch, acbramley, quietone)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3331158: Support full-screen editing in CKEditor
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Major task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: da9a867 · +66/-0 · 10 files
  • Discussion: #3331158 · 30 contributors · 66 comments

Summary

Drupal now supports fullscreen editing in CKEditor 5, a feature that was available in CKEditor 4 but missing after the upgrade to CKEditor 5. Content editors can now expand the editor to fill their entire browser window, providing more space to work with complex content and see more of their text at once. This is especially helpful when working in narrow admin themes or when editing long documents. Site administrators can add the fullscreen button to any text format toolbar through the standard CKEditor configuration interface.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

This change registers CKEditor 5's fullscreen plugin, which has been available in CKEditor 5 core since version 45.0.0, as a Drupal plugin. The implementation adds a new ckeditor5_fullscreen entry to ckeditor5.ckeditor5.yml that wraps the upstream fullscreen.Fullscreen plugin. It includes the fullscreen JavaScript library in core.libraries.yml pointing to assets/vendor/ckeditor5/fullscreen/fullscreen.js, and provides an admin library with CSS to style the toolbar button icon.

The fullscreen plugin requires no configuration beyond adding it to a text format's toolbar. It adds no HTML elements to content, so elements: false is set in the plugin definition. An SVG icon (fullscreen-enter.svg) is provided for the toolbar button, styled through fullscreen.admin.css. The feature was requested shortly after CKEditor 5 became the default editor in Drupal 10, but implementation was postponed waiting for upstream to add the functionality. CKEditor eventually released the feature as part of their core distribution, making integration straightforward.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: January 4, 2023
  • Committed: February 13, 2026 (3 years later)

Key contributors

  • nchase
  • witeksocha (CKSource)
  • smustgrave (Mobomo)
  • el7cosmos (PreviousNext)
  • mark_fullmer (University of Texas at Austin)
  • godotislate
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)

Collaboration

This issue remained open for over two years, from January 2023 when CKEditor 5 became the default until upstream finally released the fullscreen feature in late 2024. Multiple contributors noted the accessibility and usability implications for content editors working in constrained spaces. el7cosmos created the merge request that ultimately landed. mark_fullmer updated the issue summary to clarify the user interface changes. rkoller tested accessibility implications and identified upstream issues to be addressed by CKEditor, helping ensure the feature didn't block on concerns that weren't Drupal's to solve. longwave committed the change after confirming those concerns should be handled upstream.

Lessons learned

  • When a widely-requested feature becomes available upstream but is temporarily provided through a contrib module, add it to core once it stabilizes upstream. Users shouldn't need to install additional modules for functionality that's part of the upstream library's core release. (via bnjmnm, catch, longwave)
  • When identifying UX or accessibility issues in a feature that comes from an upstream library, file those issues upstream rather than patching them in the integration layer. The root cause should be fixed where the feature is implemented. (via longwave, rkoller)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3340978: Remove uses of “illegal” from core
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 5d2be21 · +34/-36 prod · +58/-58 test · +2/-0 infra · 56 files
  • Discussion: #3340978 · 7 contributors · 20 comments

Summary

Drupal core now uses "invalid" instead of "illegal" throughout the codebase to describe incorrect or unsupported values. This language change makes error messages clearer and removes unintended legal connotations. The word "illegal" has been flagged in the spell checker to prevent future use. This affects user-facing messages, developer documentation, code comments, and internal class properties across multiple subsystems including entity queries, field storage, user validation, and Views.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The change replaces all instances of "illegal" with "invalid" across approximately 50 locations in Drupal core. This includes user-facing validation messages, code comments, exception messages, and internal property names.

Key changes include renaming the UserNameConstraint::$illegalMessage property to UserNameConstraint::$invalidMessage and updating UserNameConstraintValidator to reference the new property name. Error messages in SqlContentEntityStorageSchema now say "Invalid initial value definition" and "Reserved field column names" instead of "Illegal." Views validation messages for field identifiers changed from "This identifier has illegal characters" to "This identifier has invalid characters."

The change also updates test fixtures, including renaming a test directory from project-with-illegal-dir-scaffold to project-with-invalid-dir-scaffold. The word "illegal" was added to core/.cspell.json as a flagged word to prevent future usage. Code comments in transliteration, responsive image, and entity query subsystems were updated for consistency.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 10, 2023
  • Committed: February 13, 2026 (3 years later)

Key contributors

  • schlaukopf (Globant)
  • xjm (Agileana)
  • quietone (PreviousNext)
  • ghost of drupal past
  • dcam
  • dww (3281d Consulting)
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)

Collaboration

quietone drove this issue forward, making the initial commits and adding the spell checker flag to prevent future usage of the term. dcam provided thorough text review for grammar and accuracy across the many changed strings. dww performed detailed validation of the remaining instances and confirmed the scope was appropriate, noting that migrate fixtures and vendor files were correctly excluded. The ghost of drupal past provided historical context about documentation standards and the original entity query implementation.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #2655794: Remove redundant WAI-ARIA role attributes from <main>, <nav>, <aside>, <header>, and <footer> elements
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal bug report
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 3fd5767 · +170/-170 prod · +8/-8 test · 86 files
  • Discussion: #2655794 · 20 contributors · 66 comments

Summary

Drupal core templates no longer include redundant WAI-ARIA role attributes on HTML5 semantic elements. Modern browsers automatically expose the correct accessibility roles for elements like <main>, <nav>, <aside>, <header>, and <footer>, making explicit role attributes unnecessary. This change resolves HTML validation warnings and cleans up markup without affecting accessibility. The explicit role attributes were originally added to support Internet Explorer 11, which Drupal no longer supports.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

This change removes explicit role attributes from HTML5 semantic elements in Twig templates throughout Drupal core. Modern browsers map these elements to their implicit ARIA landmark roles: <main> maps to role="main", <nav> to role="navigation", <aside> to role="complementary", <header> to role="banner", and <footer> to role="contentinfo".

The redundant attributes were originally added for Internet Explorer 11, which failed to pass implicit landmark roles to accessibility APIs. With Drupal 10+ dropping IE11 support, these attributes became unnecessary markup that triggered HTML validation warnings.

Templates affected include page.html.twig, maintenance-page.html.twig, install-page.html.twig, breadcrumb.html.twig, pager.html.twig, toolbar.html.twig, and menu block templates across the Claro, Olivero, Umami, and starterkit themes. JavaScript selectors in mobile.install.js were updated from header[role="banner"] to header. Test assertions checking for role attributes were also updated.

The Stable 9 theme was intentionally excluded from these changes to preserve backward compatibility for sites using CSS or JavaScript selectors that target role attributes.

Upgrade

If your custom theme overrides any of the affected core templates and includes custom CSS or JavaScript that selects elements by their role attribute (e.g., header[role="banner"]), update those selectors to use the element name alone or add a class selector instead:

// Before
const header = document.querySelector('header[role="banner"]');

// After
const header = document.querySelector('header');

See the change record for the complete list of affected templates.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: January 24, 2016
  • Committed: February 13, 2026 (10 years later)

Key contributors

  • BarisW (ronder)
  • mgifford (OpenConcept Consulting Inc.)
  • feyp (werk21)
  • pk188 (OpenSense Labs)
  • andrewmacpherson (Annertech)
  • mherchel
  • gauravvvv (Axelerant)
  • akshay kashyap (gai Technologies Pvt Ltd)
  • liam morland (OpenPlus)
  • dcam
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)
  • bnjmnm (Acquia)

Collaboration

This issue was opened by BarisW in January 2016 and postponed for nearly a decade until Drupal could drop Internet Explorer 11 support. Andrew Macpherson provided crucial technical research about browser accessibility API behavior and advocated for waiting until IE11 support ended. Mike Gifford championed the accessibility improvements and helped confirm the change was safe for modern assistive technologies. Liam Morland revived the issue after IE11 support was dropped and created the merge request. Mike Herchel expanded the scope to cover all affected templates across Olivero and Claro themes. Benjamin Mullins confirmed that Stable 9 should be excluded to preserve backward compatibility.

Lessons learned

  • When HTML5 semantic elements have implicit ARIA roles, explicitly declaring the same role is redundant once all supported browsers correctly expose those implicit roles to assistive technologies. However, explicit roles may still be necessary to work around accessibility bugs in specific browsers, even if they cause validation warnings. (via andrewmacpherson)
  • When fixing markup issues in stable themes, weigh the benefits against breaking CSS or JavaScript selectors that rely on the existing attributes. Even technically redundant markup should not be changed in stable themes if it provides no user-facing improvement and could cause regressions. (via longwave, bnjmnm)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3554220: Claro's libraries don't enforce the variables.css dependency
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal bug report
  • Version: 11.3.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 0d04616 · +111/-3 · 3 files
  • Discussion: #3554220 · 4 contributors · 20 comments

Summary

Claro's CSS custom properties (variables) were not loaded when contrib modules used individual Claro libraries like claro/claro.drupal.dialog or claro/claro.jquery.ui without loading the full theme. This caused broken styling when modules like gin_toolbar tried to use Claro components on non-Claro front-end themes. CSS rules that referenced variables like --jui-dropdown-fg-color or --input-border-radius-size failed to resolve, resulting in elements with missing colors, borders, and shadows.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

Claro's css/base/variables.css file defines CSS custom properties used throughout the theme's component stylesheets. Previously, this file was only loaded as part of the claro/global-styling library. When contrib modules referenced individual Claro libraries like claro/claro.jquery.ui or claro/claro.drupal.dialog, those components would load but their CSS custom property references would fail because variables.css wasn't loaded.

The fix creates a new claro/variables library definition containing only css/base/variables.css. This file is removed from claro/global-styling and instead added as a dependency to global-styling and to all 19 other Claro libraries that reference CSS custom properties. These include claro.jquery.ui, claro.drupal.dialog, drupal.tablesort, system.admin, icon-link, drupal.shortcut, status.report, views, views_ui, card, file, media_library.theme, media_library.ui, progress, filter, form-two-columns, and image.admin.

This approach allows individual Claro components to be used independently while ensuring the CSS variables they depend on are always available. The change is non-disruptive because the variables are still loaded in the same order for sites using the full Claro theme.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: October 24, 2025
  • Committed: February 12, 2026 (3 months later)

Key contributors

  • codebymikey (Zodiac Media)
  • smustgrave (Mobomo)
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)

Collaboration

codebymikey identified the issue when using gin_toolbar with non-Claro themes and created the fix. They systematically audited all Claro CSS files to identify which libraries needed the variables dependency. smustgrave reviewed the change and confirmed it was non-disruptive. longwave committed the fix and backported it to Drupal 11.3.x as an eligible bug fix.

Lessons learned

  • When creating reusable component libraries that use CSS custom properties (variables), expose the variables as a separate library dependency. Components that reference variables will fail silently if the variables aren't defined, causing broken styling when the component is used in isolation. (via codebymikey)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3568566: Olivero's secondary menus inoperable when authenticated & BigPipe present
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Major bug report
  • Version: main
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: b638f42 · +333/-231 · 3 files
  • Discussion: #3568566 · 5 contributors · 18 comments

Summary

Drupal 11.3 introduced a regression where dropdown menus in the Olivero theme stopped working for logged-in users when BigPipe was enabled. The problem occurred because Olivero's navigation JavaScript ran only on initial page load, but BigPipe loads some content asynchronously after the page appears. This meant the menu code never initialized when the navigation arrived late. The fix ensures the menu code runs whenever new content is added to the page, not just once at startup.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The second-level-navigation.js file in Olivero initialized event listeners immediately when the script loaded, querying for menu elements with document.querySelectorAll() at the top level. When BigPipe rendered the main menu block asynchronously, those elements did not exist yet, so no listeners attached.

The fix wraps the initialization in a Drupal behavior (Drupal.behaviors.secondLevelNav). The attach() method uses once() to find the primary navigation element and passes it to a new init(navElement) function. This function queries for second-level menus within the provided context and attaches all event listeners.

The document-level listeners for Escape key and outside clicks are now wrapped in their own once('olivero-second-level-nav-document', document) call. This prevents duplicate handlers if the behavior attaches multiple times. The secondLevelNavMenus variable changed from a const initialized at load to a let initialized within init().

The issue was traced to #3437499, which added placeholdering for more blocks in Drupal 11.2. This caused the main menu to render via BigPipe on cache misses, exposing the timing issue that had always existed in Olivero's non-behavior-based code.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: January 20, 2026
  • Committed: February 12, 2026 (22 days later)

Key contributors

  • mherchel (Dripyard)
  • mandclu (Acquia)
  • godotislate
  • lauriii (Acquia)

Collaboration

Mike Herchel identified the regression and created the initial patch after mandclu reported the issue on Slack. Lauri Eskola reviewed the code and improved the implementation by renaming variables to avoid shadowing, adding documentation to the init() function, and wrapping document-level listeners with once() to prevent duplicate handlers. Catch connected the dots back to the placeholdering changes that exposed this timing issue.

Lessons learned

  • When JavaScript modifies DOM elements that may be injected after page load (via BigPipe, AJAX, or similar), wrap initialization code in Drupal.behaviors so it runs both on page load and when new content arrives. Direct execution only runs once and misses dynamically inserted elements. (via mherchel, lauriii)
  • When attaching document-level event listeners inside a Drupal.behavior that may execute multiple times, wrap them with once() to prevent duplicate handlers from being registered each time the behavior is invoked. (via lauriii)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #1503146: Aliased paths cannot be set as front page
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal bug report
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 859cc28 · +144/-88 prod · +102/-6 test · +0/-12 infra · 14 files
  • Discussion: #1503146 · 30 contributors · 120 comments

Summary

Drupal's front page configuration now accepts and stores path aliases directly, making configuration portable across environments. Previously, entering a path alias like /home would automatically convert it to /node/1 in configuration, creating a hard dependency between site configuration and specific content IDs. This made it difficult to export configuration and deploy it to other environments where the same content might have different node IDs. Now the system stores whatever path you enter, whether it's an alias or an internal path, and correctly recognizes it as the front page in all cases.

Impact

Affects site owners.

  • Config change: The front page, 403, and 404 path settings in system.site config now store path aliases as entered instead of converting them to internal paths.

Technical details

The change has two main parts. First, SiteInformationForm no longer converts path aliases to system paths when saving. The form previously used AliasManager::getPathByAlias() to resolve aliases before storing them in system.site config. This conversion has been removed, and the form now uses #config_target to store values directly as entered.

Second, the PathMatcher::isFrontPage() method was not checking for aliases, so it would fail to recognize the front page when an alias was stored in config. A new AliasPathMatcher class now decorates the core PathMatcherInterface service in the path_alias module. This decorator extends isFrontPage() to check both the internal path (via the original implementation) and the alias (via a new isAliasFrontPage() method). The alias check uses AliasManager::getAliasByPath() to get the current page's alias and compares it to the configured front page path.

This decouples configuration from content IDs, which is important for configuration management workflows where content may have different IDs across environments but maintain consistent aliases. The same approach applies to the 403 and 404 error page settings.

The SiteInformationForm was simplified by removing the AliasManagerInterface dependency and the submitForm() method. The form validation no longer pre-processes paths before validation.

Upgrade

If you previously relied on system.site config always containing internal paths like /node/123, you may need to update code that reads this configuration. The front page, 403, and 404 paths may now be either internal paths or aliases. Use the path validator or alias manager to handle both cases.

When you save the site information form after this update, whatever path you enter will be stored as-is in configuration. If you have custom code that sets these values programmatically, you can now use aliases directly instead of needing to resolve them to system paths first.

See the change record for more details: #3572707

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: March 26, 2012
  • Committed: February 11, 2026 (13 years later)

Key contributors

  • pendashteh
  • gambry (EPAM)
  • raphaeltbm
  • dieterholvoet (Minsky)
  • damienmckenna (Mediacurrent)
  • rodrigoaguilera (Ymbra)
  • neclimdul
  • anruether (Geeks4Change)
  • amateescu
  • joachim (Gulp Digital)
  • catch (Third and Grove)
  • phenaproxima (Acquia)
  • godotislate
  • mstrelan
  • alexpott (Acro Commerce)
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)

Collaboration

This 14-year-old issue saw contributions from 30 people before reaching a solution. DieterHolvoet created the merge request with the decorator pattern that became the foundation of the fix. The final approach was refined through discussion among alexpott, catch, longwave, and phenaproxima, who recognized that while this change introduces some fragility (path aliases can be deleted), it doesn't make the system more fragile than it already was—you can already delete views and entities referenced by config. The team decided the benefits for configuration management workflows outweighed the risks, especially since follow-up issues were opened to add status report warnings for broken paths and to validate multilingual alias consistency.

Lessons learned

  • When configuration references content by system paths (like /node/123), it creates a hard coupling between configuration and content that breaks modern deployment workflows. Store user-facing aliases instead, and resolve them at runtime so configuration remains portable across environments where content IDs differ. (via dieterholvoet, gambry)
  • When a form auto-converts user input before saving (like converting aliases to system paths), users cannot see what value is actually stored. Display transformations can hide the stored value, but the underlying data should match user intent to avoid surprising behavior and deployment issues. (via raphaeltbm, dieterholvoet)
  • When checking if the current page matches a configured path, compare both the direct value and its resolved form. Users may configure either aliases or system paths, and runtime matching must handle both to avoid fragile exact-match comparisons. (via gambry, dieterholvoet)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3481627: Update user creation form to hide password and status fields when user is notified
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: d426572 · +34/-14 prod · +20/-20 test · 8 files
  • Discussion: #3481627 · 12 contributors · 55 comments

Summary

The manual user creation form now defaults to emailing new users with password setup instructions. When this option is checked, the password and account status fields are automatically hidden, since users will set their own password through a one-time login link sent by email. Administrators can still uncheck the box to manually set passwords and account status when needed. This matches how most modern web platforms handle user invitations and eliminates the need to create temporary passwords that are never used.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The AccountForm class in the user module has been modified to improve the user creation workflow. The notify checkbox has been moved earlier in the form, renamed to "Email user with password setup instructions" for clarity, and now defaults to checked when an administrator is creating a new account ($admin_create is true).

When the notify checkbox is checked, the password confirmation fields and status radio buttons are hidden using Form API #states. This prevents administrators from needing to enter a password that will never be used, since the user receives a one-time login link to set their own password. The status field is also hidden because notifying a user about a blocked account creates a confusing experience.

The change only affects the manual user creation form at /admin/people/create. Programmatic user creation through the API is unchanged. Tests in UserRolesAssignmentTest and the Nightwatch drupalCreateUser command were updated to explicitly set notify to FALSE when they need to create users with known passwords. The kernel test UserAccountFormFieldsTest was simplified to remove positional index checking that was no longer needed.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: October 18, 2024
  • Committed: February 11, 2026 (1 year later)

Key contributors

  • pameeela (Technocrat)
  • xjm (Zoocha)
  • lauriii (Acquia)
  • poker10 (ActivIT s.r.o.)
  • catch (Third and Grove)
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)
  • dpi (PreviousNext)
  • alexanderpas
  • neomenlo
  • clemens.tolboom
  • sergiur (University of Cambridge)
  • lostcarpark

Collaboration

pameeela opened the issue in October 2024 and shepherded it through extensive discussion about whether the default should change and whether a configuration option was needed. lauriii contributed the UX design showing how the form should behave with dynamic field visibility. catch and longwave provided release management perspective, agreeing that the change was acceptable for a minor release given that ProfileForm is explicitly marked @internal and the behavior change is clearly visible in the UI. xjm raised important concerns about the disruption of changing email defaults, ultimately acknowledging these represented a minority use case. sergiur created the change record documenting the changes for site builders.

Lessons learned

  • When implementing conditional form fields, hiding irrelevant fields based on user choices creates clearer interfaces than showing disabled fields. If a user chooses email-based account setup, hiding manual password and status fields prevents confusion about which values will be used. (via lauriii, pameeela)
  • When changing default form behavior in minor releases, the visibility of the change on the form itself determines disruption level. A checkbox whose default value changes but remains clearly visible and adjustable is less disruptive than hidden configuration changes, even if the behavioral outcome differs. (via catch, longwave, pameeela)
  • When proposing behavioral changes that affect different audiences differently, defaulting to the most common use case (80%) is appropriate even when the minority use case has stronger consequences. Document the change prominently so affected users can adapt. (via lauriii, xjm)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3559628: Allow #[MapQueryParameter] attribute for query string handling in controllers
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Version: main
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 43c2d5e · +172/-222 · 4 files
  • Discussion: #3559628 · 3 contributors · 14 comments

Summary

Drupal now supports mapping query string parameters directly to controller method arguments using the #[MapQueryParameter] attribute from Symfony 6.3. This eliminates repetitive validation code that checked for missing query parameters and manually extracted values from the request object. Controllers become shorter and easier to read, with clearer method signatures that show what parameters they expect.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • New API: Controllers can now use the #[MapQueryParameter] attribute to automatically map query string parameters to method arguments.

Technical details

This change adds Symfony's QueryParameterValueResolver to Drupal's argument resolver chain in core.services.yml. The resolver automatically extracts query string parameters and maps them to controller method arguments decorated with the #[MapQueryParameter] attribute.

ThemeController was refactored to demonstrate this pattern. The three methods uninstall(), install(), and setDefaultTheme() previously accepted a Request object, manually extracted the theme query parameter, and validated its presence. Now they accept a string $theme parameter with the #[MapQueryParameter] attribute. The validation code and throws of AccessDeniedHttpException were removed.

Error handling changed slightly. When a required query parameter is missing, the response code changed from 403 to 500, with a more helpful error message from Symfony explaining which argument could not be resolved. This is acceptable because these are error conditions that should not occur during normal operation.

Controllers are explicitly excluded from Drupal's backward compatibility policy, so this method signature change does not require deprecation.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: November 24, 2025
  • Committed: February 11, 2026 (2 months later)

Collaboration

The discussion between longwave and alexpott about the 403 to 500 response code change shows good judgment about error handling in practice. Rather than treating the status code change as a blocker, they recognized that these error conditions indicate malformed requests that should not happen during normal site operation, and that the new error messages are actually more helpful for debugging. This pragmatic assessment kept the issue moving forward while identifying a related bug in willInstallExperimentalTheme() for follow-up work in #3572785.

Lessons learned

  • When query parameter validation moves from explicit checks to framework-level attribute mapping, errors change from 4xx client errors to 5xx server errors. This is acceptable for error conditions that should never occur in normal operation and improves the specificity of error messages. (via alexpott, longwave)
  • When framework features eliminate boilerplate validation code, the improved developer experience and reduced complexity outweigh concerns about changing error response codes in edge cases that shouldn't occur during normal operation. (via alexpott, longwave)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3567544: Alias lookup by path is not case sensitive but AliasPrefixList and AliasRepository::preloadPathAlias's return value is leading to inconsistencies
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal bug report
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: cd54e64 · +78/-2 prod · +42/-0 test · +24/-0 infra · 10 files
  • Discussion: #3567544 · 4 contributors · 16 comments

Summary

Drupal's path alias system had an inconsistency where looking up aliases by system path worked correctly regardless of case (treating /node/1 and /NODE/1 the same), but internal components did not consistently honor this case-insensitive behavior. This led to situations where the alias manager would fail to find an alias when given a path with different capitalization, even though Drupal paths are supposed to be case-insensitive. The fix ensures all parts of the path alias system consistently treat paths as case-insensitive, so aliases resolve correctly regardless of how users or code capitalize the path.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The path alias system uses AliasPrefixList to cache which path prefixes have aliases and AliasRepository::preloadPathAlias() to bulk-load aliases. The bug was that AliasPrefixList performed case-sensitive lookups while AliasRepositoryInterface::lookupBySystemPath() performed case-insensitive ones, creating inconsistent behavior depending on which API was used.

The fix modifies AliasPrefixList to normalize all paths to lowercase using mb_strtolower() in its get(), set(), has(), resolveCacheMiss(), and loadMenuPathRoots() methods. This ensures the prefix cache correctly handles paths regardless of case.

In AliasRepository::preloadPathAlias(), the fix adds logic to track a mapping between user-provided paths and their lowercase equivalents. When the database returns results, if the stored path differs by case from the user-provided path, the method now returns entries for both the stored path and the user-provided path, with the stored path listed first. The interface documentation for AliasRepositoryInterface::preloadPathAlias() was updated to document this dual-entry behavior.

New test coverage was added to AliasTest to verify case-insensitive behavior in testLookupPath() and testPrefixList(), including edge cases where different capitalizations are looked up in sequence.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: January 14, 2026
  • Committed: February 10, 2026 (27 days later)

Key contributors

  • alexpott (Acro Commerce)
  • catch (Third and Grove)
  • godotislate
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3567159: Fix "expects array<string>" issues detected by phpstan
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: main
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: a7b81e9 · +33/-33 prod · +12/-12 test · 18 files
  • Discussion: #3567159 · 3 contributors · 17 comments

Summary

Drupal's codebase now passes stricter PHPStan type analysis for array parameters. The change fixes 25 type declaration errors where functions expected arrays of strings but received arrays with integers or multidimensional arrays. Most fixes update documentation comments to reflect the actual data structures used. In one case, integer values are explicitly cast to strings before being passed to str_replace(). This is part of ongoing work to improve Drupal's static analysis coverage and code quality.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

This issue resolved PHPStan violations detected when enabling the checkFunctionArgumentTypes parameter. PHPStan flagged 25 locations where @param annotations declared array<string> or string[] but the actual data structures were different.

The fixes fall into two categories. Most changes update @param documentation to accurately describe complex array structures. For example, AnnotatedClassDiscovery::$pluginNamespaces changed from string[] to array<string, list<string>> to reflect that it's a keyed array where each value is itself a list of strings. Similarly, test method parameters in ConfigEntityValidationTestBase now declare array<string, array<string, list<string>|string>>|null to describe nested validation error structures.

The other category involves runtime type casting. In Result::render(), the Views result area plugin builds a replacements array for str_replace(). Since str_replace() expects array<string>, integer values like $start, $end, and $total are now cast to strings using (string) before assignment. The final str_replace() call was also simplified to pass $replacements directly instead of calling array_values(), since the array is already properly structured.

These changes only affect internal type documentation and do not alter runtime behavior. The fixes improve PHPStan's ability to catch type errors as Drupal moves toward higher analysis levels.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: January 13, 2026
  • Committed: February 10, 2026 (28 days later)

Lessons learned

  • When fixing multiple static analysis violations across a codebase, fix as many issues as possible before adding the new rule to the baseline. This keeps violations discoverable by line number rather than requiring baseline regeneration to locate them. (via mstrelan)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3572527: \Drupal\DrupalInstalled::VERSIONS_HASH is not consistent for the same set of packages
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Critical bug report
  • Version: 11.3.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: f905d6d · +21/-3 prod · +411/-0 test · 9 files
  • Discussion: #3572527 · 3 contributors · 12 comments

Summary

Drupal generates a version hash to track when installed packages change. This hash was inconsistent because Composer does not always return packages in the same order. Identical sets of packages could produce different hashes, breaking cache invalidation and causing unnecessary rebuilds. This particularly affected multi-architecture Docker builds and other scenarios where packages are installed multiple times. The fix ensures packages are always sorted before calculating the hash, making the result predictable and reliable.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The \Drupal\DrupalInstalled::VERSIONS_HASH constant is generated by DrupalInstalledTemplate::getCode() during Composer operations. This hash is calculated from all installed packages and used to invalidate caches when dependencies change.

The bug occurred because \Composer\Repository\RepositoryInterface::getPackages() does not guarantee a consistent sort order. Running composer remove and composer require on the same package could reorder the list, changing the hash even when the final package set was identical.

The fix adds a usort() call in DrupalInstalledTemplate::getCode() that sorts packages by their unique name using the spaceship operator before passing them to array_reduce(). This ensures the concatenated version string used for hashing always follows the same order.

A new test DrupalInstalledTest was added to verify hash consistency. The test installs packages, adds two fixtures, removes one, then re-adds it, confirming the hash returns to its previous value when the package set is restored.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 10, 2026
  • Committed: February 10, 2026

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3569328: Modernize locale.translations.inc
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: b4ee668 · +1088/-376 prod · +18/-20 test · 38 files
  • Discussion: #3569328 · 4 contributors · 39 comments

Summary

The Locale module's translation functions have been converted to modern services. This modernization reduces circular dependencies in the codebase and follows Drupal's architectural direction of moving procedural code into object-oriented services. The change affects how translation sources are built and managed internally but does not change how translations work for site owners.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • Deprecation: Functions locale_translation_get_projects(), locale_translation_clear_cache_projects(), locale_translation_load_sources(), locale_translation_build_sources(), locale_translation_source_check_file(), locale_translation_source_build(), locale_translation_build_server_pattern() deprecated; underscore functions _locale_translation_file_is_remote(), _locale_translation_source_compare(), _locale_translation_default_update_options() and locale_cron_fill_queue() deprecated; constants LOCALE_TRANSLATION_SOURCE_COMPARE_LT, LOCALE_TRANSLATION_SOURCE_COMPARE_EQ, LOCALE_TRANSLATION_SOURCE_COMPARE_GT deprecated; entire locale.translation.inc file deprecated
  • New API: New LocaleSource service provides translation source building methods; new LocaleDefaultOptions class provides translation update options; LocaleProjectStorageInterface::getProjects() added

Technical details

The locale.translation.inc file contained 11 procedural functions at the bottom of a chain of circular dependencies. This change converts them to two new services and deprecates the entire file.

The new LocaleSource service provides methods for building and checking translation sources: loadSources(), buildSources(), sourceCheckFile(), sourceBuild(), and buildServerPattern(). The LocaleProjectStorageInterface gains a new getProjects() method that replaces the static-cached locale_translation_get_projects() function. The LocaleDefaultOptions class provides a static updateOptions() method for translation import options.

The locale_cron_fill_queue() function's logic was moved directly into LocaleHooks::cron() to eliminate an unnecessary layer. The _locale_translation_source_compare() function was inlined in TranslationStatusForm where it had only one usage, simplifying from a comparison constant system to direct timestamp comparison.

All deprecated functions in locale.translation.inc forward to the new service methods. Non-API functions (those prefixed with underscore) are removed in Drupal 12, while public API functions are removed in Drupal 13. The LocaleProjectStorage service now maintains its own projectCache property separate from the drupal_static system. A backward compatibility layer in drupal_static_reset() forwards calls for locale_translation_get_projects to the new service method.

Upgrade

Module developers using deprecated locale translation functions should update to the new services:

// Old:
$projects = locale_translation_get_projects();
locale_translation_clear_cache_projects();
$sources = locale_translation_build_sources($projects, $langcodes);

// New:
$projects = \Drupal::service('locale.project')->getProjects();
\Drupal::service('locale.project')->resetCache();
$sources = \Drupal::service(LocaleSource::class)->buildSources($projects, $langcodes);

For translation update options:

// Old:
$options = _locale_translation_default_update_options();

// New:
use Drupal\locale\LocaleDefaultOptions;
$options = LocaleDefaultOptions::updateOptions();

See the change record at https://www.drupal.org/node/3569330 for complete details.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: January 24, 2026
  • Committed: February 9, 2026 (16 days later)

Key contributors

  • nicxvan (nLightened Development LLC)
  • dcam
  • claudiu.cristea (Webikon)

Collaboration

nicxvan opened this issue and shepherded it through 39 comments and multiple iterations over two weeks. They adapted the approach based on feedback, first adding an enum structure then simplifying to remove unused constants, and navigated changing deprecation policies during the review process. dcam provided thorough review across multiple iterations, validating moved code, performing extensive manual testing of translation downloads and status checks, and suggesting the simplification that led to removing the enum and unused constants. Claudiu Cristea ensured credits were collected from a related duplicate issue. Berdir provided feedback on the drupal_static deprecation handling. Catch performed the final review and committed the changes.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3567616: Move _help_search_update to hook
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: e35a5a0 · +64/-82 · 4 files
  • Discussion: #3567616 · 3 contributors · 11 comments

Summary

The Help module's internal code structure has been modernized by moving a helper function into a class method and simplifying duplicated hook implementations. The change removes the procedural function _help_search_update() from help.module and consolidates four similar hook implementations into three. This is part of ongoing work to align Drupal core with modern PHP practices and make code easier to maintain. Site behavior remains identical when installing or uninstalling modules and themes.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The _help_search_update() procedural function has been removed from help.module and converted to a protected searchUpdate() method within the HelpHooks class. This function ensures the help search index is updated when extensions are installed or uninstalled.

Four hook implementations now call searchUpdate() instead of the old function: modulesInstalled(), modulesUninstalled(), and a new combined method. The themesInstalled() and themesUninstalled() hook implementations were identical except for their hook attribute, so they have been merged into a single themesInstallOrUninstall() method that uses both #[Hook('themes_installed')] and #[Hook('themes_uninstalled')] attributes.

The entire help.module file has been deleted since it only contained the helper function. All hook logic now lives in the HelpHooks class, following Drupal's modern hook system architecture. The functionality remains unchanged: when modules or themes are installed or uninstalled, the help search plugin instance updates its topic list and index state.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: January 15, 2026
  • Committed: February 6, 2026 (22 days later)

Key contributors

  • dcam

item.title: #3567163: Move field_ui form callback to hook class
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: d49ef26 · +46/-16 · 4 files
  • Discussion: #3567163 · 4 contributors · 13 comments

Summary

Drupal is modernizing its codebase by organizing form callbacks into dedicated hook classes. The Field UI module now uses an object-oriented approach for handling the "Save and manage fields" button submission instead of a standalone procedural function. This is part of a broader effort to make Drupal's code more consistent and maintainable, but does not change how the Field UI works or looks.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • Deprecation: Procedural function field_ui_form_manage_field_form_submit() deprecated in favor of FieldUiHooks::manageFieldFormSubmit() method

Technical details

The field_ui_form_manage_field_form_submit() procedural function has been moved into the FieldUiHooks class as the manageFieldFormSubmit() method. The logic remains identical: it checks if the triggering element is the save_continue button and redirects to the field overview page if appropriate.

The FieldUiHooks::formAlter() method now references the new method using the self::class . ':manageFieldFormSubmit' syntax instead of the string function name. The old procedural function remains as a deprecated wrapper that triggers an E_USER_DEPRECATED error and delegates to the new method through the service container.

This change aligns with Drupal's ongoing migration to hook classes, consolidating related hook implementations and form callbacks into cohesive class structures. The form submission handler logic itself is unchanged.

Upgrade

If your custom code references field_ui_form_manage_field_form_submit() directly as a form submit handler, update it to use the class method instead:

// Old:
$form['#submit'][] = 'field_ui_form_manage_field_form_submit';

// New:
$form['#submit'][] = \Drupal\field_ui\Hook\FieldUiHooks::class . ':manageFieldFormSubmit';

See #3566774 for full details.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: January 13, 2026
  • Committed: February 6, 2026 (24 days later)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3566792: Deprecate remaining ckeditor5.module procedural code
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: main
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 90f9762 · +510/-270 prod · +46/-6 test · 10 files
  • Discussion: #3566792 · 5 contributors · 25 comments

Summary

Drupal is eliminating procedural code from module files. This change moves the last procedural functions from the CKEditor 5 module into object-oriented methods on a hooks service class. The functions remain callable for now but will trigger deprecation warnings. This change only affects contrib modules that directly call these functions, which is rare. Most sites will see no impact.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • Deprecation: Three ckeditor5.module procedural functions deprecated: ckeditor5_filter_format_edit_form_submit(), _update_ckeditor5_html_filter(), and _ckeditor5_theme_css() moved to Ckeditor5Hooks service.

Technical details

The change moves three procedural functions from ckeditor5.module to methods on the Ckeditor5Hooks service class:

ckeditor5_filter_format_edit_form_submit() becomes Ckeditor5Hooks::filterFormatEditFormSubmit(). This form submission handler updates the allowed HTML tags when manually editable tags or styles are configured.

_update_ckeditor5_html_filter() becomes Ckeditor5Hooks::updateCkeditor5HtmlFilter(). This AJAX callback handler updates multiple areas of the text format form when switching editors or changing CKEditor 5 settings.

_ckeditor5_theme_css() becomes Ckeditor5Hooks::themeCss() and is marked protected with no replacement. This internal function retrieves theme stylesheets for use within CKEditor 5.

The deprecated procedural functions now call the new methods via dependency injection. All internal references (form callbacks, AJAX callbacks, and hook implementations) updated to use the class methods directly. The Ckeditor5Hooks constructor gains four new dependencies: RendererInterface, ConfigFactoryInterface, ThemeExtensionList, and MessengerInterface to support the moved logic.

This change is part of the broader initiative to eliminate .module files from Drupal core.

Upgrade

Only contrib modules that directly call the deprecated functions need to update:

If you call ckeditor5_filter_format_edit_form_submit(), inject the Ckeditor5Hooks service and call filterFormatEditFormSubmit() instead.

If you call _update_ckeditor5_html_filter() as a form callback, change the callback reference to Ckeditor5Hooks::updateCkeditor5HtmlFilter.

If you call _ckeditor5_theme_css(), this was an internal function with no direct replacement. The CKEditor Premium module is known to use it and will need an alternative approach.

See the change record at #3570104 for details.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: January 10, 2026
  • Committed: February 5, 2026 (25 days later)

Collaboration

Five contributors collaborated on this issue over 25 comments. The team consolidated three overlapping issues into one to avoid conflicts. During review, the team identified that the CKEditor Premium module calls _ckeditor5_theme_css(), prompting discussion about whether to deprecate or delete the function. The decision to deprecate rather than delete follows the standard practice for this initiative, minimizing disruption even when replacement paths are unclear. This demonstrates thoughtful consideration of downstream impacts during API changes.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3571492: Several tests fail on PHP 8.1 on 10.6.x because of requirements issues
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Critical bug report
  • Version: 10.6.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: f739f7a · +140/-12 · 20 files
  • Discussion: #3571492 · 3 contributors · 14 comments

Summary

Several functional tests were failing on PHP 8.1 in Drupal 10.6.x because they incorrectly used a trait method designed to handle PHP version warnings. The tests were calling methods to address requirements problems that did not actually exist in their specific test scenarios. This issue will recur when Drupal 11 sites begin upgrading to PHP 8.5 in preparation for Drupal 12.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The RequirementsPageTrait provides methods to handle situations where Drupal displays requirements warnings during installation or updates, such as when running on older PHP versions. Several test classes were incorrectly calling updateRequirementsProblem() or not properly overriding setUpRequirementsProblem() for their specific test scenarios.

The fix removes incorrect calls to updateRequirementsProblem() from EquivalentUpdateTest where the tests are focused on testing equivalent update logic, not requirements checking. For tests like InstallerBrokenDatabaseCredentialsTest and InstallerBrokenDatabasePortSettingsTest that intentionally test requirements problems, the fix adds empty setUpRequirementsProblem() overrides to prevent the trait's default behavior from interfering.

StandardRecipeInstallTest received two changes. It now disables CSS preprocessing via system.performance configuration to ensure the Claro admin theme can be correctly detected by assertRequirementSummaries(). It also overrides setUpRequirementsProblem() because recipe installation bypasses the normal requirements checking flow.

InstallerExistingConfigNoProfileTest also adds an empty setUpRequirementsProblem() override since this test scenario does not involve requirements checking.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Committed: February 4, 2026

  • longwave (Full Fat Things)

item.title: #3463524: editor_post_update_sanitize_image_upload_settings fails on missing text format
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Critical bug report
  • Version: 10.6.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 5e89bf9 · +32/-4 prod · +50/-0 test · 5 files
  • Discussion: #3463524 · 7 contributors · 25 comments

Summary

When upgrading from Drupal 10.2.x to 10.3.x or later, sites could fail with a fatal error during database updates if an editor configuration entity referenced a text format that no longer existed. This happened when modules like Webform were uninstalled but left orphaned editor configurations behind. The update process would crash with "Call to a member function getConfigDependencyName() on null," blocking the upgrade. This fix adds defensive checks so the update completes with a warning instead of failing, letting site owners clean up the orphaned configuration later.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The editor_post_update_sanitize_image_upload_settings() post-update hook failed when an Editor entity's format property pointed to a non-existent FilterFormat entity. This happened in Editor::calculateDependencies() when calling $this->getFilterFormat()->getConfigDependencyName() - if getFilterFormat() returned NULL, the chained method call triggered a fatal error.

The fix adds a null check in Editor::calculateDependencies(). When getFilterFormat() returns NULL, the code now skips adding the config dependency and emits a E_USER_WARNING via trigger_error() with a message identifying the orphaned editor configuration. The warning message tells site owners which editor entity is misconfigured and suggests reviewing whether it's still needed.

This scenario occurred when modules like Webform created text formats and editor configurations, then were uninstalled. The text format would be deleted but the editor configuration could remain. The new code handles this gracefully so updates can complete.

A unit test was added to EditorConfigEntityUnitTest to verify that when getFilterFormat() returns NULL, the warning is triggered and no config dependency is added.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: July 24, 2024
  • Committed: February 4, 2026 (1 year later)

Key contributors

  • sboden
  • catch (Third and Grove)
  • alexpott (Acro Commerce)
  • quietone (PreviousNext)

Collaboration

This issue affected multiple sites upgrading to 10.3.x, with the reporter encountering it on three separate instances. The root cause was identified through collaboration between the reporter and maintainers who recognized the pattern from similar issues. The reporter helped diagnose by discovering that orphaned Webform editor configurations were the trigger. The fix was committed to Drupal 10.6.x, 11.x, 11.3.x, and the development branch. A follow-up issue was opened to add additional test coverage for newer branches where PHPUnit's warning expectation API changed.

Lessons learned

  • When config entities reference other entities, the referenced entity may no longer exist (e.g., after module uninstallation). Code calculating dependencies should check for null and handle the missing dependency gracefully with logging rather than fataling, preventing upgrade path failures. (via catch, alexpott)
  • When a fatal error occurs during database updates on a stable release, bump the issue to critical priority since the upgrade path affects all users updating to that version, especially when triggered by common scenarios like installing and uninstalling popular contributed modules. (via catch)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3570133: PHPUnit security update
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Critical task
  • Version: 10.6.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: ac069df · +180/-156 prod · +3/-3 test · 19 files
  • Discussion: #3570133 · 6 contributors · 32 comments

Summary

Drupal core updated its PHPUnit dependency to address a security vulnerability. This is a development-only dependency used for running tests. The vulnerability exists in PHPUnit itself and does not affect production sites. Site owners running custom tests should update their development dependencies to receive the security fix. The update brings PHPUnit to version 11.5.50 on Drupal 11.x, 11.3.x, and 10.6.x branches (which use PHPUnit 9.6.34).

Impact

Affects site owners.

  • Security: PHPUnit security vulnerability CVE-2026-24765 addressed

Technical details

This issue updates the phpunit/phpunit constraint in composer.json from ^11.5.42 to ^11.5.50 on main, 11.x, and 11.3.x branches, and from ^9.6.13 to ^9.6.34 on the 10.6.x branch. These versions contain fixes for CVE-2026-24765, a security vulnerability in PHPUnit.

Running composer audit on affected branches would have flagged this vulnerability. The update was performed by running composer update -W phpunit/phpunit to allow updating dependencies with the -W flag, then manually updating the constraint in both the root composer.json and core/tests/PHPStan/composer.json files, followed by composer update --lock to regenerate lock files.

The update also pulls in updated versions of related packages including phpunit/php-code-coverage (11.0.12), phpunit/php-file-iterator (5.1.1), sebastian/comparator (6.3.3), and nikic/php-parser (5.7.0) as required by the dependency tree. These are all development-only dependencies with no impact on production code.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: January 28, 2026
  • First commit: February 2, 2026 (5 days later)
  • Last commit: February 4, 2026 (1 day later)

Key contributors

  • kentr
  • catch (Third and Grove)
  • smustgrave (Mobomo)
  • mondrake
  • larowlan (PreviousNext)
  • dww (3281d Consulting)

Collaboration

This issue moved quickly through the process, from opening to first commit in five days. The security team approved handling this as a public issue. Multiple contributors collaborated on creating merge requests for different branches when cherry-picking proved unsuccessful. The team prioritized getting this into the next patch release window, which was seven days away when the issue was bumped to critical priority. The final commits required coordinating the order of merges across branches to avoid unnecessary rebases.
